attributes.stem:
A two-week-old girl presents to the Emergency Department with her parents due to a rapidly enlarging red lesion around her right eye noticed since birth. She was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. Her parents report she is feeding well and meeting developmental milestones.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a 3 cm bright red, raised vascular lesion confined to the superficial skin of the right upper eyelid causing partial closure of the eye. The lesion blanches with pressure. The globe position is normal and there is no proptosis. Eye movements are full and the remainder of the examination including cardiac examination is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial investigation?
options: Magnetic resonance imaging of the head and neck
Computed tomography scan of the head
attributes.explanation:
This infant presents with a likely infantile haemangioma confined to the superficial skin of the periocular region. The clinical features of a bright red, compressible lesion that blanches with pressure and has grown rapidly since birth are characteristic. The absence of proptosis or globe displacement suggests no deeper orbital involvement. Most infantile haemangiomas undergo spontaneous involution, with approximately 80% resolved by eight years of age.

Ultrasound of the orbit is the most appropriate initial investigation for superficial infantile haemangiomas. It is readily available, non-invasive, does not require sedation, and can effectively demonstrate the extent and vascularity of superficial lesions. Ultrasound typically shows irregular hyperechoic structures and allows monitoring of the lesion's response to treatment if required.

Magnetic resonance imaging provides excellent soft tissue resolution but would be excessive for a superficial lesion without clinical features suggesting deeper involvement. MRI would be considered if ultrasound demonstrated concerning features or if there were signs of orbital involvement requiring more detailed assessment.

Computed tomography involves unnecessary radiation exposure and would not provide additional useful information for a superficial lesion. CT should be avoided in infants when possible due to the risks of ionising radiation, particularly given the availability of safer alternatives.

Plain radiograph of the skull would not provide useful information about the soft tissue components of the haemangioma or its relationship to surrounding structures. Plain films have no role in the evaluation of suspected infantile haemangiomas.

Fluorescein angiography is an invasive procedure that would not be appropriate as an initial investigation in an infant. While it can provide information about retinal vasculature, it is not indicated for evaluation of superficial periocular haemangiomas and would require sedation in this age group.
learningPoints: Infantile haemangiomas typically present as bright red, compressible lesions that grow rapidly in the first few months of life.
Early evaluation of periocular haemangiomas is essential as they can cause visual axis obstruction leading to amblyopia.
Ultrasound is the first-line imaging modality for superficial periocular haemangiomas without features suggesting deeper extension.
references: Joseph J, Wain T. Infantile haemangiomas: Identifying high-risk lesions in primary care. Aust J Gen Pract. 2021 Dec;50(12):887–91. Available at: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2021/december/infantile-haemangiomas
Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne. Haemangioma – infantile. Published 2023 Dec. Available at: https://www.rch.org.au/kidsconnect/prereferral_guidelines/Haemangioma_%E2%80%93_infantile/
